draft.introduction: The Odyssey opens by invoking the Muse to tell of the man of many turns (polytropos), Odysseus, who wandered far after the sack of Troy. It establishes the poem’s themes of cunning, endurance, and homecoming.

translation.text: Tell me, O Muse, of the man of many ways, who was driven far and wide after he had sacked the sacred city of Troy. Many were the men whose cities he saw and whose mind he learned, and many the woes he suffered in his heart upon the sea, seeking to win his own life and the return of his companions.
translation.original_text: ἄνδρα μοι ἔννεπε Μοῦσα πολύτροπον ὃς μάλα πολλὰ πλάγχθη

translator_notes.note: The epithet polytropos (πολύτροπον) means “of many turns”—both literally (much-traveled) and metaphorically (cunning, versatile). Where the Iliad’s hero Achilles embodies force, the Odyssey’s hero embodies intelligence and adaptability.
